source.title: Protein design and variant prediction using autoregressive generative models

outline.heading: Results
outline.preview:
# Results An autoregressive generative model of biological sequences. Protein sequences observed in organisms today result from mutation and selection for functional, folded proteins over time scales of a few days to a billion years. Generative models can be used to parameterize this view of evolution. Namely, they express the probability that a sequence x would be generated by evolution ![](../../mineru_output/0056_
...[clipped]...

outline.heading: Discussion
outline.preview:
# Discussion Here we show how neural network-powered generative auto-regressive models can be used to model sequence constraints independent of alignments and design novel functional sequences for previously out-of-reach applications such as nanobodies. The capability of these models is based on demonstrated state-of-the-art performance and on an extended range of applicability in the space of sequences. In the parti
...[clipped]...

outline.heading: Methods
outline.preview:
# Methods Model. Sequences are represented by a 21-letter alphabet for proteins or a five-letter alphabet for RNAs, one for each residue type and a “start/stop” character. Training sequences are weighted inversely to the number of neighbors for each sequence at a minimum identity of 80%, except for viral families, where a 99% identity threshold was used, as was done previously $^{30}$ . Sequence sets are derived from
...[clipped]...

outline.preview: # Data availability All data generated and analyzed during the study are available in this published article, its supplementary information files, and on the github repository (https://github.com/debbiemarkslab/SeqDesign; https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4606785 $^{104}$ ).

outline.heading: Code availability

Received: 28 February 2021; Accepted: 26 March 2021; Published online: 23 April 2021

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of designing functional protein sequences and predicting variant effects, particularly for applications where multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) are not robust, such as antibodies with hypervariable CDRs, disordered proteins, and indels. The goal is to develop an alignment-free autoregressive generative model that can learn evolutionary constraints from natural sequences, generate diverse functional sequences, and predict mutation effects without relying on alignments.

section_gists.heading: Protein design and variant prediction using autoregressive generative models
section_gists.gist: Introduces the problem: current state-of-the-art methods rely on MSAs, which are inadequate for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels. Proposes an alignment-free autoregressive deep generative model adapted from NLP to predict variant effects and design functional sequences. Highlights the challenge of antibody design, where large synthetic libraries contain many non-functional proteins. The model aims to learn constraints from natural sequences to design smart, diverse, and functional nanobody libraries.
process_signals: Motivation: MSA-based methods fail for variable-length CDRs, disordered proteins, and indels.
Hypothesis: Autoregressive models can capture higher-order sequence constraints without alignments.
Goal: Design a 10^5 nanobody library that outperforms larger synthetic libraries in expression.
methods: Autoregressive generative model (dilated convolutional neural networks)
Likelihood ratio for mutation effect prediction
failures_or_limits: MSA-based methods are unsuitable for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
Large synthetic libraries contain many non-functional proteins.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes the autoregressive model architecture and its application. The model uses dilated CNNs to avoid exploding gradients seen with LSTMs/GRUs. It is first validated on mutation effect prediction across 40 deep mutational scans, showing state-of-the-art performance. The model is then applied to design a nanobody library: 33M CDR3 sequences generated, filtered to 3.7M, and clustered to select 185,836 diverse, high-probability sequences. The designed library shows better expression than a 1000-fold larger synthetic library.
process_signals: Encountered exploding gradients with LSTM/GRU, switched to dilated CNNs.
Model validated on mutation effect prediction before design.
Designed library outperforms synthetic library in expression.
methods: Dilated convolutional neural networks with residual blocks
Log-ratio of likelihoods for mutation effect prediction
metrics: Spearman correlation for mutation effect prediction
failures_or_limits: Generated sequences filtered for glycosylation sites, deamination motifs, and sulfur-containing amino acids.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the model's state-of-the-art performance on mutation effect prediction and its extended applicability to sequences without robust alignments (indels, disordered proteins). Highlights the potential for analyzing disease-associated variants in disordered regions. The designed nanobody library shows higher mean expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed variants compared to a synthetic library. A specific designed nanobody (Nb174684) binds HSA with high affinity, demonstrating functional design.
process_signals: Model performance on par with best available methods.
Designed library has 0.16 poorly expressed vs 0.26 for synthetic.
Nb174684 shows specific HSA binding, validating functional design.
failures_or_limits: Semi-supervised methods risk overfitting due to data leakage; unsupervised model avoids this.
Model applicability to non-coding regions, promoters, enhancers, and genomes is speculative.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details the model architecture: residual causal dilated CNNs with 6 blocks of 9 layers, weight/layer normalization, L2 regularization, dropout (p=0.5), batch size 30, channel size 48. Training uses Adam for 250k updates with gradient clipping. Sequences are weighted by inverse neighbor count. For nanobody design, 33M CDR3 sequences generated, filtered, and clustered using BIRCH with k-mer featurization. Library constructed via yeast display, transformed into yeast, and characterized for expression and binding.
process_signals: Model architecture chosen to handle long sequences and avoid exploding gradients.
Library generation involved multiple filtering and clustering steps to ensure diversity and fitness.
methods: Yeast display library construction and characterization
failures_or_limits: Generated sequences filtered for undesirable motifs (glycosylation, deamination, sulfur-containing).

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from the limitation of MSA-based methods for certain protein classes to the development of an alignment-free autoregressive model.
Initial attempts with LSTM/GRU failed due to exploding gradients, leading to the adoption of dilated CNNs.
The model was first validated on mutation effect prediction to build confidence before applying to design.
The nanobody library design involved iterative filtering and clustering to balance fitness and diversity.
Wet-lab validation confirmed that the designed library outperforms a larger synthetic library in expression and can yield functional binders.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does the model handle very long sequences or multiple protein families?
What are the limits of diversity in generated sequences?
Can the model be applied to other protein engineering tasks beyond nanobodies?
How does the model perform on predicting effects of indels in disordered regions compared to other methods?
What is the scalability of the library generation and screening process?

events.summary: Current computational methods rely on multiple sequence alignments, which are inadequate for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
An autoregressive generative model adapted from NLP can predict and design functional sequences without alignments.
The model is designed to capture higher-order sequence constraints by learning from natural sequences to design smart, diverse nanobody libraries.
Dilated convolutional neural networks were used instead to parameterize the autoregressive likelihood, avoiding gradient issues.
The model was first validated on mutation effect prediction across 40 deep mutational scans, showing state-of-the-art performance.
Alignment-based methods are inherently unsuitable for variable-length CDRs, disordered proteins, and indels.

events.summary: The autoregressive model matches state-of-art alignment-dependent missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 deep mutational scan experiments.
The autoregressive model predicts nanobody thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
The model predicts binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with insertions, deletions, and missense mutations with AUC 0.90.
After filtering, 185,836 diverse CDR3 sequences are selected for the designed library.
The designed nanobody library shows 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing nanobodies than the synthetic library.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains validation of mutation effect prediction, indel prediction, and experimental results of designed nanobody library.

events.summary: Designed library shows higher mean expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies compared to synthetic library.
Designed library contains functional nanobodies that bind HSA, with one binder showing moderate affinity.
Model validated on deep mutational scan data, showing on-par performance with best available methods.
Semi-supervised methods risk overfitting due to data leakage; unsupervised model avoids this.
Prediction of indels and mutation effects in disordered regions is difficult, despite prevalence in disease.
Poly-reactivity remains a challenge; training on mature B cell repertoires may improve library design.

events.summary: Generated 33M CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling from the autoregressive model.
Filtered generated sequences to remove duplicates, training set matches, and those with undesirable motifs.
Constructed nanobody library by cloning CDR3 sequences into yeast display plasmid and transforming yeast.
Isolated and characterized a specific nanobody (Nb174684) that binds HSA with high affinity.
Memory constraint of 70 GB limited clustering parameter choices.

This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and license information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
We introduce a deep generative model adapted from natural language processing for prediction and design of diverse functional sequences without the need for alignments.

Instead of experimentally producing unnecessarily massive, largely non-functional libraries, we can design smart libraries of fit and diverse nanobodies for the development of highly specific and possibly therapeutic nanobodies.

Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field
We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
alignment-based methods are inherently unsuitable for the statistical description of the variable length, hypermutated complementarity determining regions (CDRs) of antibody sequences
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.

The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).
Accurate prediction of binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with a range of insertions, deletions, and missense mutations of the autoregressive model (blue), higher than HMM (orange).
From these, we select 185,836 highly diverse CDR3 sequences for inclusion in our designed library.
Upon induction, the designed nanobody library contained a 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing and displaying nanobodies on their cell surface than the synthetic nanobody library
the designed library had nearly half the fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies (cells with fluorescence below 10,000 AU) as compared to the synthetic library
the designed library has almost double the mean expression level (166,193 AU compared to 92,183 AU)
we selected nanobody sequences that bound to human serum albumin (HSA) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model
great care must be taken in the way the split between training and test is conducted to evaluate the true generalizability of the method
Our model is not subject to these limitations as its training is fully unsupervised.
the prediction of insertions and deletions and mutation effects in disordered regions has been a difficult research area, despite their prevalence in human genomes
A challenge of using synthetic libraries is the poly-reactivity of many sequences
training a model on sequences from mature or memory B cell repertoires may provide information on how to improve library design
we generated 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling

Using this model, we find that the log-probability fitness calculations predict the thermostability of unseen llama nanobody sequences from four different stability experiments $^{69-72}$ (Fig.

Interestingly, the accuracy of effect predictions against the aliphatic amidase mutation scan is remarkably robust even with a low number of training sequences—123 non-redundant sequences provide the same accuracy as 36,000—suggesting that there is more to learn about the relationship between evolutionary sampling and model learning.

Previously published benchmarks $^{29}$ demonstrate the higher accuracy of the probabilistic models, EVmutation compared to SIFT and PolyPhen and recent work demonstrate that DeepSequence outperforms all currently available methods when measured against experimental mutation scans $^{34}$ .

Somewhat surprisingly, unsupervised, generative models trained only on evolutionary sequences are proving the most accurate for predicting the effect of mutations when compared to large datasets of experimentally measured mutation effects $^{30,34}$ , and they avoid the risk of overfitting that can occur as a result of circularity in supervised methods $^{68}$ .

We then trained our validated statistical method on naïve nanobody repertoires $^{57}$ as naïve antibody repertoires have been shown to have functional sequences with the capacity to target diverse antigens $^{58}$ and used it to generate probable sequences.

source.title: Protein design and variant prediction using autoregressive generative models

initial_request.research_problem: Current computational methods rely on multiple sequence alignments, which are inadequate for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
initial_request.known_context: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
constraints: No reliance on multiple sequence alignments
Must handle variable length CDRs, insertions, deletions
input_data: Deep mutational scan data (40 experiments, 690,257 sequences)
quantifiable_goals: Match or exceed alignment-based methods in mutation effect prediction
Design a nanobody library with higher expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed variants than a synthetic library

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alignment-based methods fail for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No alignment-free method exists that can predict mutation effects and design functional sequences for these challenging cases.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An autoregressive generative model adapted from NLP can predict and design functional sequences without alignments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a deep generative model based on autoregressive factorization of sequence probability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model can learn from natural sequences to capture higher-order constraints.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the model architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
We introduce a deep generative model adapted from natural language processing for prediction and design of diverse functional sequences without the need for alignments.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to implement model.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to parameterize the autoregressive likelihood to capture long-range dependencies in protein sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Dilated convolutional neural networks can aggregate long-range dependencies without gradient issues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use dilated CNNs instead of LSTM/GRU.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dilated CNNs avoid exploding gradients and capture long-range dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate model on mutation effect prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model architecture chosen; need to test predictive performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the alignment-free model can match alignment-based methods on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can predict mutation effects as accurately as DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 40 deep mutational scan datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model matches state-of-the-art alignment-based method DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on nanobody thermostability and indel fitness.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on missense mutations; need to test on antibody-related tasks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict nanobody thermostability better than HMMs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can learn from natural nanobody repertoires to predict thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train on ~1.2M llama nanobody sequences and test on thermostability data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model predicts thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test on indel fitness prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model works for missense and stability; need to test on indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict fitness effects of insertions and deletions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can handle variable-length mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on IGP dehydratase dataset with indels and missense mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC = 0.90, outperforming HMM (AUC 0.88).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequence generation for nanobody library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Accurate prediction of binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with a range of insertions, deletions, and missense mutations of the autoregressive model (blue), higher than HMM (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated; ready to design nanobody library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a diverse set of functional CDR3 sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ancestral sampling from the trained model can produce probable CDR3 sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 33M CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter and cluster sequences to select a diverse library.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to filter out duplicates, training set matches, and sequences with problematic motifs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Applying filters for glycosylation sites, deamination motifs, and sulfur-containing amino acids will improve library quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Remove sequences with NxS/NxT, NG, C, M; also remove duplicates and training set matches.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 3,690,554 sequences remain after filtering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cluster sequences to select a diverse subset.

evidence.quote_or_span: CDR3 sequences were also removed if they contained glycosylation (NxS and NxT) sites, asparagine deamination (NG) motifs, or sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine and methionine), resulting in 3,690,554 sequences.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 3.7M filtered sequences; need to select a manageable diverse set.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to cluster sequences to maximize diversity within memory constraints?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BIRCH clustering with chosen k-mer size and diameter threshold can select diverse sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use BIRCH clustering with parameters chosen under 70 GB memory constraint.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 185,836 diverse CDR3 sequences selected for the designed library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Synthesize library and test expression in yeast.

evidence.quote_or_span: From these, we select 185,836 highly diverse CDR3 sequences for inclusion in our designed library.
K-mer size and BIRCH diameter threshold were chosen to maximize the number of clusters within a memory constraint of 70 GB.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library of 185,836 sequences; need to compare with synthetic library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the designed library show improved expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed library will have higher mean expression and fewer poorly expressed variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize oligonucleotide library, clone into yeast display vector, and measure expression via flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed library: mean expression 165,000 AU, poorly expressed fraction 0.16; Synthetic: 90,000 AU, 0.26.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test functionality by selecting binders against HSA.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon induction, the designed nanobody library contained a 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing and displaying nanobodies on their cell surface than the synthetic nanobody library
the designed library had nearly half the fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies (cells with fluorescence below 10,000 AU) as compared to the synthetic library
the designed library has almost double the mean expression level (166,193 AU compared to 92,183 AU)
| Designed library | 165000 | 0.16 | 0.
| Synthetic library | 90000 | 0.26 | 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library shows improved expression; need to confirm functional binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the designed library contain nanobodies that can bind a target antigen?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The library contains functional binders; one can be isolated against HSA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform FACS against HSA, isolate clones, and characterize binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Isolated nanobody Nb174684 binds HSA with Kd 9.8 μM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected nanobody sequences that bound to human serum albumin (HSA) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
Nanobody Nb174684 was isolated from a screen of 36 clones for binding to HSA using a flow cytometer and then sequenced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional binder obtained; but poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce poly-reactivity in designed libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on mature B cell repertoires may improve library design by reducing poly-reactivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and suggest future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Poly-reactivity is a challenge; training on mature B cell repertoires may help.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: A challenge of using synthetic libraries is the poly-reactivity of many sequences
training a model on sequences from mature or memory B cell repertoires may provide information on how to improve library design

validation_methods: Mutation effect prediction on 40 deep mutational scans
success_verification.final_conclusion: The autoregressive model successfully predicts mutation effects and designs a nanobody library with improved expression and functional binders, though poly-reactivity remains a challenge.

findings.problem: Step 1 claims to cover K09 (generation of 33M CDR3 sequences) but this event occurs much later in the trajectory (order 9). The step is about hypothesis formulation, not sequence generation. This misalignment indicates a wrong order/causality.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K09 from the supporting_event_ids of step 1. Step 1 should only cover K01 and K02.

findings.problem: Step 4 claims to cover K08 (IGP dehydratase indel fitness) and K09 (generation of 33M CDR3 sequences), but these events are not part of the nanobody thermostability validation. K08 is a separate validation step (order 8) and K09 is a later design step (order 9). This misrepresents the trajectory order.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K08 and K09 from the supporting_event_ids of step 4. Step 4 should only cover K07.

findings.problem: Step 5 claims to cover K07 (nanobody thermostability) but this event was already covered in step 4. The step is about indel fitness prediction (K08), so K07 should not be included.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K07 from the supporting_event_ids of step 5. Step 5 should only cover K08.

findings.problem: Step 6 claims to cover K02 (hypothesis) and K03 (design goal), but these are early events (orders 2 and 3) and not directly related to the generation of 33M CDR3 sequences. The step should focus on K09.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K02 and K03 from the supporting_event_ids of step 6. Step 6 should only cover K09.

findings.problem: Step 3 claims to cover K07 (nanobody thermostability) but this validation occurs later (order 7). The step is about mutation effect prediction (K06), so K07 should not be included.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K07 from the supporting_event_ids of step 3. Step 3 should only cover K06.

findings.problem: Step 4 claims to cover K02 (hypothesis) but this is an early event (order 2) and not directly part of the nanobody thermostability validation. The step should focus on K07.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K02 from the supporting_event_ids of step 4. Step 4 should only cover K07.

findings.problem: Step 5 claims to cover K02 (hypothesis) but this is an early event (order 2) and not directly part of the indel fitness validation. The step should focus on K08.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K02 from the supporting_event_ids of step 5. Step 5 should only cover K08.

findings.problem: Step 6 claims to cover K11 (clustering to 185,836 sequences) but this event occurs later (order 11). The step is about generating 33M sequences (K09), so K11 should not be included.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K11 from the supporting_event_ids of step 6. Step 6 should only cover K09.

findings.problem: Step 7 claims to cover K11 (clustering to 185,836 sequences) but this event occurs later (order 11). The step is about filtering to 3.7M sequences (K10), so K11 should not be included.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K11 from the supporting_event_ids of step 7. Step 7 should only cover K10.

findings.problem: Step 10 claims to cover K12 (expression comparison) but this event was already covered in step 9. The step is about functional validation (K13), so K12 should not be included.
findings.revision_instruction: Remove K12 from the supporting_event_ids of step 10. Step 10 should only cover K13.

self_critic.summary: The candidate trajectory has multiple errors in the supporting_event_ids, where steps claim to cover events that are out of order or not directly related to the step's phase. This misrepresents the causality and order of the scientific process. The revision should correct these mappings to align with the canonical event graph.

source.title: Protein design and variant prediction using autoregressive generative models

initial_request.research_problem: Current computational methods rely on multiple sequence alignments, which are inadequate for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
initial_request.known_context: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
constraints: No reliance on multiple sequence alignments
Must handle variable length CDRs, insertions, deletions
input_data: Deep mutational scan data (40 experiments, 690,257 sequences)
quantifiable_goals: Match or exceed alignment-based methods in mutation effect prediction
Design a nanobody library with higher expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed variants than a synthetic library

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alignment-based methods fail for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No alignment-free method exists that can predict mutation effects and design functional sequences for these challenging cases.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An autoregressive generative model adapted from NLP can predict and design functional sequences without alignments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a deep generative model based on autoregressive factorization of sequence probability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model can learn from natural sequences to capture higher-order constraints.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the model architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
We introduce a deep generative model adapted from natural language processing for prediction and design of diverse functional sequences without the need for alignments.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to implement model.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to parameterize the autoregressive likelihood to capture long-range dependencies in protein sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Dilated convolutional neural networks can aggregate long-range dependencies without gradient issues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use dilated CNNs instead of LSTM/GRU.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dilated CNNs avoid exploding gradients and capture long-range dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate model on mutation effect prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model architecture chosen; need to test predictive performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the alignment-free model can match alignment-based methods on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can predict mutation effects as accurately as DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 40 deep mutational scan datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model matches state-of-the-art alignment-based method DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on nanobody thermostability and indel fitness.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on missense mutations; need to test on antibody-related tasks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict nanobody thermostability better than HMMs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can learn from natural nanobody repertoires to predict thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train on ~1.2M llama nanobody sequences and test on thermostability data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model predicts thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test on indel fitness prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model works for missense and stability; need to test on indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict fitness effects of insertions and deletions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can handle variable-length mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on IGP dehydratase dataset with indels and missense mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC = 0.90, outperforming HMM (AUC 0.88).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequence generation for nanobody library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Accurate prediction of binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with a range of insertions, deletions, and missense mutations of the autoregressive model (blue), higher than HMM (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated; ready to design nanobody library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a diverse set of functional CDR3 sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ancestral sampling from the trained model can produce probable CDR3 sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 33M CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter and cluster sequences to select a diverse library.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 33M sequences generated; need to remove undesirable ones.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to filter out duplicates, training set matches, and sequences with problematic motifs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Applying filters for glycosylation sites, deamination motifs, and sulfur-containing amino acids will improve library quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Remove sequences with NxS/NxT, NG, C, M; also remove duplicates and training set matches.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 3,690,554 sequences remain after filtering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cluster sequences to select a diverse subset.

evidence.quote_or_span: CDR3 sequences were also removed if they contained glycosylation (NxS and NxT) sites, asparagine deamination (NG) motifs, or sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine and methionine), resulting in 3,690,554 sequences.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 3.7M filtered sequences; need to select a manageable diverse set.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to cluster sequences to maximize diversity within memory constraints?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BIRCH clustering with chosen k-mer size and diameter threshold can select diverse sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use BIRCH clustering with parameters chosen under 70 GB memory constraint.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 185,836 diverse CDR3 sequences selected for the designed library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Synthesize library and test expression in yeast.

evidence.quote_or_span: From these, we select 185,836 highly diverse CDR3 sequences for inclusion in our designed library.
K-mer size and BIRCH diameter threshold were chosen to maximize the number of clusters within a memory constraint of 70 GB.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library of 185,836 sequences; need to compare with synthetic library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the designed library show improved expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed library will have higher mean expression and fewer poorly expressed variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize oligonucleotide library, clone into yeast display vector, and measure expression via flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed library: mean expression 165,000 AU, poorly expressed fraction 0.16; Synthetic: 90,000 AU, 0.26.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test functionality by selecting binders against HSA.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon induction, the designed nanobody library contained a 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing and displaying nanobodies on their cell surface than the synthetic nanobody library
the designed library had nearly half the fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies (cells with fluorescence below 10,000 AU) as compared to the synthetic library
the designed library has almost double the mean expression level (166,193 AU compared to 92,183 AU)
| Designed library | 165000 | 0.16 | 0.
| Synthetic library | 90000 | 0.26 | 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library shows improved expression; need to confirm functional binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the designed library contain nanobodies that can bind a target antigen?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The library contains functional binders; one can be isolated against HSA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform FACS against HSA, isolate clones, and characterize binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Isolated nanobody Nb174684 binds HSA with Kd 9.8 μM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected nanobody sequences that bound to human serum albumin (HSA) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
Nanobody Nb174684 was isolated from a screen of 36 clones for binding to HSA using a flow cytometer and then sequenced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional binder obtained; but poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce poly-reactivity in designed libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on mature B cell repertoires may improve library design by reducing poly-reactivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and suggest future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Poly-reactivity is a challenge; training on mature B cell repertoires may help.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: A challenge of using synthetic libraries is the poly-reactivity of many sequences
training a model on sequences from mature or memory B cell repertoires may provide information on how to improve library design

validation_methods: Mutation effect prediction on 40 deep mutational scans
success_verification.final_conclusion: The autoregressive model successfully predicts mutation effects and designs a nanobody library with improved expression and functional binders, though poly-reactivity remains a challenge.

evidence.quote_or_span: Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model architecture chosen; need to test predictive performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the alignment-free model can match alignment-based methods on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can predict mutation effects as accurately as DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 40 deep mutational scan datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model matches state-of-the-art alignment-based method DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on nanobody thermostability and indel fitness.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on missense mutations; need to test on antibody-related tasks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict nanobody thermostability better than HMMs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can learn from natural nanobody repertoires to predict thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train on ~1.2M llama nanobody sequences and test on thermostability data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model predicts thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test on indel fitness prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model works for missense and stability; need to test on indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict fitness effects of insertions and deletions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can handle variable-length mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on IGP dehydratase dataset with indels and missense mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC = 0.90, outperforming HMM (AUC 0.88).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequence generation for nanobody library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Accurate prediction of binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with a range of insertions, deletions, and missense mutations of the autoregressive model (blue), higher than HMM (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated; ready to design nanobody library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a diverse set of functional CDR3 sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ancestral sampling from the trained model can produce probable CDR3 sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 33M CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter and cluster sequences to select a diverse library.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 33M sequences generated; need to remove undesirable ones.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to filter out duplicates, training set matches, and sequences with problematic motifs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Applying filters for glycosylation sites, deamination motifs, and sulfur-containing amino acids will improve library quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Remove sequences with NxS/NxT, NG, C, M; also remove duplicates and training set matches.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 3,690,554 sequences remain after filtering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cluster sequences to select a diverse subset.

evidence.quote_or_span: CDR3 sequences were also removed if they contained glycosylation (NxS and NxT) sites, asparagine deamination (NG) motifs, or sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine and methionine), resulting in 3,690,554 sequences.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 3.7M filtered sequences; need to select a manageable diverse set.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to cluster sequences to maximize diversity within memory constraints?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BIRCH clustering with chosen k-mer size and diameter threshold can select diverse sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use BIRCH clustering with parameters chosen under 70 GB memory constraint.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 185,836 diverse CDR3 sequences selected for the designed library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Synthesize library and test expression in yeast.

evidence.quote_or_span: From these, we select 185,836 highly diverse CDR3 sequences for inclusion in our designed library.
K-mer size and BIRCH diameter threshold were chosen to maximize the number of clusters within a memory constraint of 70 GB.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library of 185,836 sequences; need to compare with synthetic library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the designed library show improved expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed library will have higher mean expression and fewer poorly expressed variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize oligonucleotide library, clone into yeast display vector, and measure expression via flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed library: mean expression 165,000 AU, poorly expressed fraction 0.16; Synthetic: 90,000 AU, 0.26.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test functionality by selecting binders against HSA.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon induction, the designed nanobody library contained a 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing and displaying nanobodies on their cell surface than the synthetic nanobody library
the designed library had nearly half the fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies (cells with fluorescence below 10,000 AU) as compared to the synthetic library
the designed library has almost double the mean expression level (166,193 AU compared to 92,183 AU)
| Designed library | 165000 | 0.16 | 0.
| Synthetic library | 90000 | 0.26 | 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library shows improved expression; need to confirm functional binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the designed library contain nanobodies that can bind a target antigen?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The library contains functional binders; one can be isolated against HSA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform FACS against HSA, isolate clones, and characterize binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Isolated nanobody Nb174684 binds HSA with Kd 9.8 μM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected nanobody sequences that bound to human serum albumin (HSA) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
Nanobody Nb174684 was isolated from a screen of 36 clones for binding to HSA using a flow cytometer and then sequenced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional binder obtained; but poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce poly-reactivity in designed libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on mature B cell repertoires may improve library design by reducing poly-reactivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and suggest future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Poly-reactivity is a challenge; training on mature B cell repertoires may help.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: A challenge of using synthetic libraries is the poly-reactivity of many sequences
training a model on sequences from mature or memory B cell repertoires may provide information on how to improve library design

validation_methods: Mutation effect prediction on 40 deep mutational scans
success_verification.final_conclusion: The autoregressive model successfully predicts mutation effects and designs a nanobody library with improved expression and functional binders, though poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
